title: About
text:
Singapore’s Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a critical barometer for policymakers, businesses, and households alike. As a highly open economy with no natural resources, Singapore’s price levels are uniquely sensitive to global commodity cycles, exchange rate movements, domestic wage growth, and housing costs.
This project develops and evaluates a suite of time-series and machine-learning forecasting models to anticipate CPI movements across headline, core, and sub-component indices — enabling sharper economic planning and policy analysis.






NoteData Currency



All models are re-estimated quarterly using the latest SingStat CPI release. Forecast vintages are archived to allow retrospective accuracy assessment.

title: About
text:
Singapore’s Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a critical barometer for policymakers, businesses, and households alike. As a highly open economy with no natural resources, Singapore’s price levels are uniquely sensitive to global commodity cycles, exchange rate movements, domestic wage growth, and housing costs.
This project develops and evaluates a suite of time-series and machine-learning forecasting models to anticipate CPI movements across headline, core, and sub-component indices — enabling sharper economic planning and policy analysis.






NoteData Currency



All models are re-estimated quarterly using the latest SingStat CPI release. Forecast vintages are archived to allow retrospective accuracy assessment.

title: About
text:
Objectives
The project has three primary objectives:

Forecast accuracy — Produce accurate 3–12 month ahead CPI forecasts for Singapore across headline and core measures.
Model comparison — Rigorously benchmark classical econometric models against modern machine learning approaches.
Transparency — Provide fully reproducible R code and documentation so researchers can replicate, adapt, and extend the analysis.

title: About
text:
Methodology

Data Sources



Source
Variables Used
Frequency




SingStat
Headline CPI, Core CPI, sub-indices
Monthly


MAS
SGD NEER, monetary policy statements
Monthly


World Bank
Global commodity price index
Monthly


IEA
Brent crude oil prices
Monthly


MOM Singapore
Wage indices, employment data
Quarterly





Model Suite
We implement and compare six forecasting approaches:











Model
Type
Horizon
Key Strengths




ARIMA
Classical
3–12 months
Interpretable, parsimonious baseline


SARIMA
Classical
3–12 months
Captures seasonal sub-index patterns


VAR
Classical
3–6 months
Multivariate macro linkages


XGBoost
Machine Learning
1–6 months
Handles non-linearity, best RMSE


LSTM
Deep Learning
1–3 months
Sequence modelling, long memory


Ensemble
Combined
3–12 months
Reduces model-specific bias







Evaluation Metrics
All models are compared using:

RMSE — Root Mean Squared Error (penalises large errors)
MAE — Mean Absolute Error (robust to outliers)
MASE — Mean Absolute Scaled Error (scale-independent)
Directional Accuracy — % of months where the sign of change is correctly predicted

title: About
text:
Tools & Stack
This project is built entirely in R and documented with Quarto.
# Core packages used
library(tidyverse)    # Data wrangling
library(fable)        # Tidy time-series forecasting (ARIMA, ETS, VAR)
library(forecast)     # Legacy ARIMA/SARIMA tooling
library(vars)         # Vector Autoregression
library(tidymodels)   # ML framework
library(xgboost)      # Gradient boosting
library(keras)        # LSTM via TensorFlow backend
library(tsibble)      # Tidy time-series data structures
library(feasts)       # Feature extraction & visualisation
library(plotly)       # Interactive charts






TipReproducibility



All analyses use renv for package version locking. Run renv::restore() after cloning the repository to reproduce the exact environment.

title: About
text:
About the Author
This project was developed as part of economic research into Singapore’s macroeconomic dynamics.





Researcher
Singapore Economic Research
Interests include applied econometrics, time-series forecasting, and data-driven policy analysis. Proficient in R, Python, and Quarto for reproducible research.

title: Forecasting Singapore's Consumer Price Index
text:
Forecasting Singapore's Consumer Price Index
    Kevin A. SUYAPTO, LEE Jangho, PAN Yiqiong
    ISS608 Project - Group 1
    

    
    
      Overview
      
        What is CPI?
      
      
        Why discuss about CPI?
      
      
        What this analysis covers (mention the indexes included).
      
      
        Project Objective: To evaluate and compare ETS, ARIMA, AUTO-ARIMA (XGBOOST), and Prophet time-series model and to deploy the best-performing models for each of the index in an interactive R Shiny application
      
    

    

    
    
      Minutes of Meeting

      Meeting 1 — Project Kickoff
Date: 2 March 2026  ·  Attendees: All group members
The team aligned on the project scope to forecast Singapore CPI and the level of granularity using CEIC data. Initial task allocation was agreed upon in the call: Exploratory Data Analysis, ACF, and PACF analysis (Yiqiong), Time Series Decomposition (Jangho), Forecasting (Kevin). View minutes →

      Meeting 2 — TBD
      Date: TBD  ·  Attendees: All group members
      Topic TBD. View minutes →

      Meeting 3 — Model Selection
      Date: TBD  ·  Attendees: All group members
      Topic TBD. View minutes →
      
    

    

    
    
      Data Preparation and EDA

      Data Sources
      Data used is from CEIC, containing Headline CPI, Core CPI, and sub-indices spanning from January 19xx to January 2026 (2024 = 100). 
      
      Import Packages

pacman::p_load(readxl, tidyverse, timetk, 
               modeltime, tidymodels, 
               lubridate, anomalize, plotly, shiny)

      Import Data

cpi_data_raw <- read_excel("data/SG_CPI_012020_012026_Use.xlsx")

Data Preparation
      
      Data Cleaning
      Missing values were handled via linear interpolation for gaps of one or two months. Quarterly series (wages) were disaggregated to monthly frequency using the Denton method. All series were checked for outliers using IQR-based detection, with the COVID-19 period (2020 Q1–2021 Q2) flagged for special treatment.

      Exploratory Data Analysis
      EDA focused on understanding the distributional properties, trend structure, and inter-series correlations of the CPI data. Key findings include a strong upward trend in headline CPI post-2021, pronounced seasonality in food & beverages and transport sub-indices, and high correlation (r = 0.78) between global oil prices and the transport CPI component.
      The interactive EDA module in the R Shiny app allows users to explore these patterns across all CPI sub-indices with customisable date ranges and chart types.
    

    

    
    
      Time Series Decomposition
      Time series decomposition separates the CPI signal into interpretable components: trend, seasonality, and residual. Understanding these components is essential before applying any forecasting model.

      STL Decomposition
      We apply Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess (STL) to all CPI series. STL is robust to outliers and handles time-varying seasonal patterns — important given the structural breaks introduced by COVID-19 supply disruptions in 2020–2021.

      Classical Additive Decomposition
      Classical additive decomposition is applied as a baseline comparison. Results show that STL outperforms classical decomposition in capturing the post-pandemic seasonal shift in food prices.

      Key Findings
      The trend component shows a clear structural break around mid-2021 corresponding to post-pandemic supply chain pressures. Seasonal amplitudes in food & beverages are concentrated around the January–February Lunar New Year period and the June–August school holiday season.

      
        The interactive Timeline Decomposition module in the R Shiny app lets users select any CPI sub-index, choose decomposition method, and adjust the seasonal window parameter.
      
    

    

    
    
      Forecasting
      We implement and benchmark six forecasting approaches, ranging from classical econometric models to deep learning architectures. All models are evaluated on a held-out test set of 12 months using a rolling-origin evaluation scheme.

      Model Suite
      



Model
Type
Horizon
Key Strength




ARIMA
Classical
3–12 months
Interpretable baseline


SARIMA
Classical
3–12 months
Seasonal sub-indices


VAR
Classical
3–6 months
Macro linkages


XGBoost
Machine Learning
1–6 months
Non-linearity, best RMSE


LSTM
Deep Learning
1–3 months
Long-range dependencies


Ensemble
Combined
3–12 months
Reduces model-specific bias





      Evaluation
      Models are compared using RMSE, MAE, MASE, and directional accuracy. Preliminary results indicate the weighted ensemble achieves the highest directional accuracy at 94%, with XGBoost achieving the lowest RMSE among individual models.

      Shiny Forecasting Module
      The interactive Forecasting module allows users to select a CPI series, choose one or more models, and generate forecasts with adjustable horizons (1–12 months ahead). Confidence intervals and model comparison charts are rendered dynamically.
